retrieved_docs.title: Funnel-Web Spider Toxicity -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Patients concerned about an FWS bite should be asked if they saw the spider that bit them. If a patient does not describe seeing a "big black spider," then FWS envenomation is less likely. A redback spider, also known as the Australian black widow, is also black but has a large red mark on its back. When evaluating the patient, the bite's geographic location should be considered.

retrieved_docs.title: Foot Drop -- Toxicokinetics
retrieved_docs.content: Chemicals and other toxins can have adverse effects on skeletal muscle and the nervous system. The effects of these agents are not always symmetrical and are beyond the intended scope of this article. However, toxic effects, along with a thorough history of chemical exposure, should be considered and obtained when evaluating any patient presenting with weakness.

retrieved_docs.title: Foot Drop -- Toxicity and Adverse Effect Management
retrieved_docs.content: If a toxic etiology is identified, the offending agent should be discontinued. Medications contributing to toxicity that cannot be stopped abruptly should be tapered gradually under medical supervision.

retrieved_docs.title: Black Widow Spider Toxicity -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Patients typically present within hours after the bite has occurred. Classically, the patient describes activities in which they have entered into the habitat of the black widow spider, such as hiking, camping, gardening, shopping, or activities in a garage. Most often, these bites occur on the patients’ extremities and often go initially unnoticed but can also be immediately associated with pain.

retrieved_docs.title: Black Widow Spider Toxicity -- Consultations
retrieved_docs.content: Consultation with a medical toxicologist is strongly encouraged.

retrieved_docs.title: Agent Orange Toxicity -- Pathophysiology -- 2,4-Dichlorophenoxyacetic Acid
retrieved_docs.content: 2,4-Dichlorophenoxyacetic Acid (2,4-D) is a chlorophenoxy herbicide marketed since the 1940s to kill broadleaf weeds. 2,4-D has generally low toxicity, and poisoning is rare. Severe toxicity requires substantial acute ingestion or chronic exposure to high herbicide concentrations. Acutely, 2,4-D is a corrosive substance that uncouples cellular oxidative phosphorylation. Poisoning classically causes gastrointestinal, muscular, and neurotoxicity. These toxic manifestations include but are not limited to acute gastritis, upper gastrointestinal bleeding, acute kidney injury, rhabdomyolysis, ataxia, hypotonia, seizures, and coma. [14] [15] [16] [17] [18] Cutaneous and inhalational exposure may cause mild gastrointestinal symptoms and progressive mixed sensory-motor peripheral neuropathy. [15] [17]

retrieved_docs.title: EMS Weapons Of Mass Destruction And Related Injury -- Issues of Concern -- Chemical
retrieved_docs.content: Small Pox (Variola major virus) has been declared to be eradicated by the WHO in 1980. Currently, two known research laboratories (the United States and Russia) have small quantities. This highly contagious virus has a latency period of 12 to 14 days. Initial symptoms are flu-like with high fever, fatigue, malaise, headache, backache, and rash. The rash starts as flat red lesions that progress to fluid-filled pustules at the same rate. Treatment is supportive, and mortality is around 30%. A vaccine and immune globulin may be provided as PEP.

retrieved_docs.title: Agent Orange Toxicity -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The planning and execution of herbicide spraying missions during Operation Ranch Hand were strict; spray missions did not occur with friendly forces in the area and were often deep in hostile territory. Antipersonnel ordinance from escort aircraft was used in targeted areas to suppress hostile ground fire, and friendly units were prohibited from entering areas immediately after spraying due to possible unexploded ordinance. [4] Multiple safeguards and strict adherence to protocols made direct spraying of United States and Allied forces, or their passage through recently sprayed areas, very unlikely. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Pediculosis Corporis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Lindane is not recommended for first-line treatment due to some potentially serious reactions including neurotoxicity and seizures. In some settings, such as refugee camps with large numbers of people, environmental application of chemical insecticides may be needed to prevent the spread of body lice and louse-borne infections. [12] [13] [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Trench Foot -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: By keeping the feet warm, dry, and clean, trench foot can be avoided. During World War I, the institution of regular foot inspections was found to decrease the incidence of the disease. The soldiers were paired up and were instructed to watch the feet of their partner. It was found that a soldier was more likely to remove his boots and dry his socks and feet if he was reminded to do so by his fellow soldier. After thousands of cases were seen, soldiers were ordered to have 3 pairs of socks with them at all times and were told to change rotate the pairs at least twice a day.   Whale oil was also provided to the soldiers to rub on their feet after they were dry to help prevent trench foot. Historically, a battalion (up to 1000 men) at the front in World War I, could use up to 10 gallons of whale oil on any given day. To help prevent trench foot, wooden boards, known as duckboards, were used to cover the wet, muddy, cold ground in the trenches. Troop rotation was also used to limit an individual soldier’s exposure. As World War I progressed, the incidence of the disease declined due to the measures mentioned above. [11] [5] [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Funnel-Web Spider Toxicity -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: The differential diagnosis for FWS bites includes other arthropod bites, cutaneous abscesses, and cellulitis. Other differentials in patients with systemic symptoms include acute coronary syndrome, anaphylaxis, congestive heart failure, organophosphate exposure, nerve agent exposure, or medication overdose. An adequate history and physical examination help determine the likelihood of FWS envenomation.

retrieved_docs.title: Biological Weapon Toxicity -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Biological weapons are agents, including bacteria, viruses, fungi, and toxins, used for malicious purposes: in war (biowarfare), to cause terror (bioterrorism), or for criminal acts (biocrime). [ Suspected Intentional Use Of Biologic And Toxic Agents. 2018 ] Some agents are lethal, and others cause illness or incapacitation. Entities direct biological agents at the human population, crops, and livestock. More than 180 pathogens and biotoxins have been researched or employed as biological weapons, including those that cause anthrax, tularemia, brucellosis, plague, Legionnaire disease, Q fever, glanders, melioidosis, smallpox, viral hemorrhagic fevers, influenza, coccidiosis, rice blast, and wheat rust. Biotoxins include ricin, botulinum toxin, and staphylococcal enterotoxin B. Designers create biological weapons to disperse as aerosols, facilitating rapid spread across large populations. However, agents may also spread from person to person or by vectors, ingestion, direct contact, or other methods.

retrieved_docs.title: Steppage Gait -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Predominantly axonal neuropathies are seen with exposure to toxins, viral or bacterial infections, chemotherapeutic agents as vincristine or vinblastin. Diabetes, alcohol intoxication, and renal disease can lead to mixed peripheral polyneuropathies.

retrieved_docs.title: Paraphenylenediamine Toxicity -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Substance abuse, such as synthetic cannabinoids, amphetamines, cocaine, and heroin Medication-induced nephrotoxicity, such as cyclosporine, vancomycin, and aminoglycosides Alcohol toxicity Occupational toxins, such as agrichemicals Heavy metal toxicity, such as calcium, lead, mercury, and uranium Snake bites, such as Viperidae and Elapidae Viral hepatitis Vascular ischemia Hypersensitivity and anaphylaxis Trauma and crush syndrome Malignant hyperthermia Metabolic myopathies Extreme physical activity-induced rhabdomyolysis Heat disorders, such as heat stroke and heat exhaustion Severe dehydration

retrieved_docs.title: Paraphenylenediamine Toxicity -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Henna is typically crushed into a paste and applied to skin, fingernails, and hair to create intricate patterns, often for festive or cultural events. [7] However, paraphenylenediamine has been increasingly added to henna paste to produce black henna. This practice decreases the amount of henna paste required, achieves a darker pigmentation, and speeds up the staining process. [2] Unfortunately, this is an unregulated adulteration of the henna paste in most locations.

retrieved_docs.title: Biological Weapon Toxicity -- Clinical Significance -- Smallpox
retrieved_docs.content: Smallpox is spread by airborne droplets or contact with body fluids. After an incubation period of 1 to 2 weeks, smallpox presents in 1 of 4 clinical forms: ordinary, modified, malignant, or hemorrhagic. Ordinary smallpox begins as a macular rash that progresses to pustules and then vesicles; lesions first present on the face and distal extremities and spread to the trunk. Unlike chickenpox, lesions in any part of the body progress simultaneously through the clinical stages.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 1 -- Community 60
retrieved_docs.content: This hierarchical knowledge base connects each causative agent to its specific poisoning type, situates those poisonings within larger accidental or unspecified psychoactive poisoning frameworks, and delineates the spectrum of organic mental disorders and intoxication syndromes that may arise, thereby aiding clinicians in agent identification, poisoning classification, and anticipation of associated neuropsychiatric manifestations for optimal patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2256
retrieved_docs.content: Disorder due to and following poisoning is a broad category of medical conditions that arise as a result of toxic exposure, and poisoning by nonmedicinal substances is recognized as a specific type within this category. This relationship indicates that any disorder classified under “Disorder due to and following poisoning” can include cases where the toxic agent is a non‑medicinal substance, helping clinicians to organize and address poisoning‑related health issues more precisely.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 1 -- Community 87
retrieved_docs.content: These interlinked relationships illustrate how drug dependence, various specific poisonings, and broader toxicity categories intersect, providing a structured framework for diagnosis, coding, prevention, and management of poisoning‑related disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 4 -- Community 2834
retrieved_docs.content: Chemically induced processes are recognized as applicable qualifiers for a range of medical conditions, including تمزق (tear), اعتلال الكلية السكري (diabetic nephropathy), فقر الدم الناتج عن نقص الحديد (iron‑deficiency anemia), and التسمم الوشيقي (septicemia). In addition, the qualifier “chemically induced” itself may be further specified by the presence of خلل الحركة المتأخر (delayed movement disorder) or by فطار غزوي (aggressive feeding). This framework highlights that certain pathologies can arise from chemical triggers and that the nature of those chemical inductions can be refined by additional clinical features, aiding precise characterization and management of affected patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 812
retrieved_docs.content: Burns, referred to in Arabic as حرق حراري, denote thermal injuries to the skin and underlying tissues; the term حرق حراري directly describes the heat‑induced nature of these lesions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 2 -- Community 620
retrieved_docs.content: overlapping nature of toxicological classifications and the importance of recognizing both acute and delayed effects, as well as intentional versus unintentional exposure, in clinical assessment and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1627
retrieved_docs.content: The toxic effect of an unspecified substance, especially one that is non‑medical in origin (التأثير السام لمادة غير محددة، خاصة غير الطبية من حيث المصدر) is classified as a subcategory of metal poisoning (التسمم بالمعادن). This relationship indicates that exposures to unidentified non‑medical agents can be understood within the broader framework of metal poisoning, which encompasses various toxic effects caused by metals and guides clinical assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 1 -- Community 213
retrieved_docs.content: are mapped onto more specific toxic entities. Finally, “Poisoning caused by biological substance” and “Poisoning due to chemical substance” are inverse‑ISA relationships to “Tryptophan poisoning,” indicating that tryptophan toxicity can arise from either biological or chemical origins. This integrated hierarchy delineates how diverse toxic agents, their pharmacologic classes, and associated clinical syndromes interrelate within medical classification systems, supporting comprehensive assessment and management of poisoning cases.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 2 -- Community 323
retrieved_docs.content: The category “Disorder due to and following poisoning” serves as a broad classification that encompasses both general poisoning (التسمم) and poisoning by non‑medicinal substances. Within this framework, the specific condition of alcohol poisoning (التسمم الكحولي) is recognized as a distinct subtype of poisoning. The subcategory “Poisonings and Drug Effects” is also placed under the umbrella of poisoning, indicating that drug‑related toxic effects are considered part of the overall poisoning spectrum. Clinical observations link poisoning (التسمم) with adrenal syndrome (متلازمة الأدرينالية), highlighting an association that may be relevant for diagnosis and management of patients presenting with toxic exposures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 1828
retrieved_docs.content: The conditions نوبة (seizure), خلل الحركة المتأخر (late‑onset movement disorder), فطار غزوي, intestinal infection, and إجهاض تلقائي غير كامل (incomplete spontaneous abortion) are all recognized as entities that can be qualified as chemically induced. Identifying a chemical trigger as a possible cause is important for the evaluation and management of these diverse clinical presentations, because exposure to certain substances may precipitate seizures, movement abnormalities, gastrointestinal infections, and incomplete abortions. Awareness of the chemically induced nature of these conditions helps clinicians consider appropriate diagnostic testing, preventive strategies, and targeted therapies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2260
retrieved_docs.content: Poisoning by drugs, medicinal and biological substances is classified as a subcategory within the broader parent category of poisoning by analgesics, antipyretics, and antirheumatics.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2266
retrieved_docs.content: Poisoning by other medications and drugs is classified as a late effect of poisoning due to a drug, medicinal, or biological substance.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 2 -- Community 279
retrieved_docs.content: Hydroxokobalamin, a hydroxylated form of vitamin B12, may be used as a therapeutic agent for the treatment of poisoning (التسمم). By binding toxic substances such as cyanide, hydroxokobalamin facilitates their conversion into non‑toxic metabolites that can be safely eliminated from the body, making it a valuable option in the management of acute toxic exposures. Its potential use in poisoning underscores the importance of recognizing specific antidotes in emergency and critical care settings.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 794
retrieved_docs.content: Poisoning / injury is a broad category that encompasses various types of harm, including superficial injury as one of its subcategories. Superficial injury, in turn, includes specific conditions such as خدش القرنية (corneal abrasion), which represents a minor injury to the surface of the eye. Recognizing this hierarchical relationship helps clinicians classify and manage injuries appropriately, from general poisoning or injury contexts down to particular superficial lesions like corneal abrasions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1621
retrieved_docs.content: Poisoning by analgesics, antipyretics, and antirheumatics is classified under the broader category of drug poisoning (التسمم بالعقاقير). This relationship indicates that adverse effects resulting from the ingestion or exposure to these common pain‑relieving, fever‑reducing, and antirheumatic medications are considered a specific subset of overall medication‑related toxicities. Recognizing this hierarchical link helps clinicians organize toxicological assessment, prioritize diagnostic evaluation, and apply appropriate treatment protocols within the general framework of drug poisoning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1566
retrieved_docs.content: Accidental nefopam poisoning is a toxic condition that results from exposure to the analgesic drug nefopam, with nefopam identified as the causative agent in these poisoning events. This relationship highlights that overdose or inadvertent administration of nefopam can lead to serious adverse effects, underscoring the importance of recognizing nefopam as the responsible agent for accurate diagnosis, appropriate treatment, and prevention of accidental toxicity.
